data.title: Why Southwest Airlines (LUV) Stock Is Falling Friday
data.description:
Southwest Airlines Co (NYSE:LUV) is trading lower Friday afternoon as investors react to a sharp escalation in the U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict, fueling worries about jet-fuel costs and demand for discretionary air travel.

Here’s what investors need to know.

  • Southwest Airlines stock is among today’s weakest performers. What’s weighing on LUV shares?

Middle East Strikes Escalate Tensions Overnight Thursday

Overnight Thursday, there were several strikes and missile incidents in the region. These included an Israeli strike on an underground bunker under Iran's supreme leader's compound in Tehran, Iranian drones and missiles aimed at the U.S. Al Udeid air base in Qatar and drones being intercepted over Saudi Arabia.

Governments in the region issued emergency alerts, and some airlines, such as Etihad Airways, were only slowly restarting limited flights.

Oil Price Fears Weigh On Southwest Airlines

For Southwest, the issue isn't direct exposure to Middle East routes because most of its flights are within the U.S., with a few nearby international routes. The bigger concern is how this new conflict could continue to push oil prices higher.

The closure of the Strait of Hormuz has already disrupted crude shipments and forced measures like a temporary waiver for India to buy stranded Russian oil, underscoring supply stress. Higher crude prices typically feed quickly into jet fuel, one of Southwest's largest operating expenses.

Fuel Spikes Challenge Southwest Low Cost Strategy

Southwest, which operates an all-Boeing 737 narrowbody fleet and targets price-sensitive leisure and small-business travelers, has limited ability to pass sudden fuel spikes through to customers without hurting demand.

At the same time, the intensifying conflict adds another macro shock for a company already managing wage inflation, fleet-delivery delays and a competitive domestic market.

LUV Stock Weakens But Holds Above 200 Day SMA

Southwest Airlines stock recently pulled back after approaching its yearly high near $54.80, with the latest decline pushing the price below its 20-day simple moving average (SMA). The drop also brought shares closer to the 50-day SMA, a key short-term support level traders watch for momentum shifts.

Despite the recent weakness tied to geopolitical tensions and fuel-cost concerns, LUV still remains above its longer-term 200-day SMA, indicating the broader uptrend has not yet fully broken.

LUV Shares Drop 6% Friday

LUV Price Action: Southwest Airlines shares were down 6.22% at $41.18 at the time of publication on Friday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: UK Growth Edges Higher In Early 2026; Stocks Poised To Benefit
data.description:
The U.K. economy entered 2026 with signs of stabilization after nearly two years of weak growth, though underlying pressures continue to weigh on the outlook.

Gross domestic product expanded by just 0.1% in the fourth quarter of 2025, highlighting how fragile the recovery remains following an extended period of near-flat economic activity. Inflation has eased to 3.0%, down from 3.4% the previous month, providing some relief for households and policymakers.

Lower inflation has allowed the Bank of England to reduce interest rates by roughly 1.5 percentage points since mid-2024, although policy remains restrictive with the benchmark rate at 3.75%.

Despite these improvements, several indicators point to persistent economic strain. Unemployment has risen to 5.2%, up roughly 331,000 year over year, while public debt stands at 92.9% of GDP, limiting the government's fiscal flexibility.

Retail sales have begun to stabilize, but consumer confidence remains in negative territory, suggesting households remain cautious amid higher borrowing costs and a still-uncertain labor market.

Taken together, the data suggests the economy has moved out of contraction, but momentum remains weak. The key question for investors is whether 2026 represents the beginning of a sustained recovery or simply a period of stabilization within a longer phase of subdued growth.

Key Sectors Driving The U.K. Economy

Structural shifts across several industries are expected to shape the U.K.'s medium-term economic trajectory. Investment flows and policy priorities suggest growth will likely be concentrated in a few strategic sectors.

Energy Transition And Infrastructure

The U.K.'s energy sector is undergoing a significant transformation as the country accelerates its shift toward lower-carbon power generation.

While North Sea oil and gas production continues to decline, investment in renewable energy, grid infrastructure and energy storage is rising. By 2026, annual investment in clean energy generation, electricity networks and energy-efficiency upgrades is estimated to exceed £40 billion.

Offshore wind remains a central component of the country's energy strategy, with the U.K. maintaining one of the largest installed capacities globally. At the same time, grid upgrades and battery storage are becoming increasingly important as electricity demand rises.

The scale of this transition positions the U.K. as a key hub for long-term investment in energy infrastructure and low-carbon power.

Advanced Manufacturing And Aerospace

Manufacturing accounts for roughly 9% of U.K. GDP, smaller than in previous decades but still strategically important in high-value sectors such as aerospace, defense and precision engineering.

The aerospace industry continues to benefit from large global aircraft order backlogs and steady demand for engines, components and maintenance services. Increased government defense spending has also supported investment in advanced manufacturing.

To remain competitive in a high-cost environment, companies in these sectors are focusing on automation, productivity improvements and export-oriented production.

Financial Services And Capital Markets

Financial services remain one of the U.K.'s most globally competitive industries, contributing more than 8% of GDP and a significant portion of tax revenues.

Despite structural adjustments following Brexit, London continues to serve as a major global financial center across asset management, insurance, foreign exchange and capital markets.

Growth in private markets, wealth management and alternative investments has supported the sector, while ongoing innovation in financial technology and digital payments continues to expand its global reach.

The sector's strong international revenue base also provides resilience during periods of slower domestic economic growth.

Technology And Digital Services

Technology remains one of the fastest-growing segments of the U.K. economy. Digital services, software development, fintech and data-driven business models continue to expand at high single-digit to low double-digit rates.

Technology exports and cross-border digital services are playing an increasingly important role in the country's services trade.

As companies invest in automation, cybersecurity and cloud infrastructure to improve efficiency, technology spending is expected to remain a structural growth driver across multiple industries.

Companies Positioned To Benefit From Structural Trends

Several U.K.-listed companies are positioned to benefit from these long-term sector developments.

RELX PLC

RELX PLC (NYSE:RELX) operates at the intersection of data analytics, technology and professional services.

Over the past decade, the company has shifted away from print publishing toward digital analytics platforms and subscription-based information services. By 2026, more than 80% of revenue is recurring and digital, supporting stable margins and predictable cash flows.

The company benefits from global demand for legal data, scientific research platforms and risk analytics, positioning it well even during periods of slower economic growth.

BP PLC

BP PLC (NYSE:BP) has undergone a major strategic repositioning as the global energy industry transitions toward lower-carbon sources.

While the company continues to operate traditional oil and gas assets, it has expanded investment into renewable energy, bioenergy and energy trading. Capital spending on low-carbon initiatives has increased significantly compared with the mid-2010s.

BP's diversified portfolio allows it to generate cash flow across varying energy price environments while gradually increasing exposure to the energy transition.

Tesco PLC

After facing margin pressure during the recent inflation surge, profitability has improved as price pressures eased and consumer demand stabilized.

The company's scale and supply chain efficiency have helped restore pricing power, making it a relatively defensive exposure within the consumer staples sector.

Outlook

The U.K. economy in 2026 appears poised for an inflection point. Inflation is declining, interest rates have eased and economic activity has stabilized after an extended period of stagnation.

However, growth remains modest, unemployment is rising and high public debt continues to constrain fiscal policy.

As a result, the path toward recovery is likely to be gradual rather than rapid. For investors, opportunities may lie less in broad economic acceleration and more in selective exposure to resilient sectors such as data-driven services, energy infrastructure, financial markets and essential consumer goods.

Whether stabilization evolves into sustained recovery will depend on policy credibility, labor market resilience and the ability of businesses and households to rebuild confidence in the years ahead.

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.

data.title: If You Invested $1000 In Shopify Stock 10 Years Ago, You Would Have This Much Today
data.description:
Shopify (NASDAQ:SHOP) has outperformed the market over the past 10 years by 35.77% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 48.73%. Currently, Shopify has a market capitalization of $171.14 billion.

Buying $1000 In SHOP: If an investor had bought $1000 of SHOP stock 10 years ago, it would be worth $51,591.87 today based on a price of $131.23 for SHOP at the time of writing.

Shopify's Performance Over Last 10 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.

data.title: Deal Dispatch: Nestlé Sells Blue Bottle; Netflix Buys Ben Affleck's InterPositive, Eddie Bauer Nixes Bankruptcy Auction
data.description:
New On The Block

  • TA Associates is reportedly selling bubble-tea maker Gong Cha. The private equity firm is reportedly working with JPMorgan Chase to explore a potential deal. The transaction could value the bubble-tea chain at around $2 billion. Talks are still in the early stages, and no decisions have been made yet.

Updates From The Block

  • Private equity firm Trive Capital has made a strategic investment in women's special occasion apparel company Adrianna Papell. The current management team will remain in place to drive the company's strategic growth initiatives. 
  • Cleco Power is drawing interest from Stonepeak Partners and Bernhard Capital Partners, as they consider purchasing the energy company from Macquarie Group, Bloomberg reports. The deal would value Cleco at more than $5 billion. Negotiations are ongoing.
  • New State Capital Partners has acquired co-working platform Vast Coworking Group as a carve-out from the United Franchise Group. Terms of the transaction remain undisclosed. Co-founder Jason Anderson will continue to serve as CEO.

Off The Block

Bankruptcy Block

  • Eddie Bauer canceled its March 6 bankruptcy auction for its 174 physical stores after failing to receive any bids for the retail operations by the deadline, Reuters reported this morning. The outdoor retailer filed for bankruptcy last month and was seeking a buyer for its brick-and-mortar stores. Eddie Bauer remains open to offers from interested buyers and will continue holding store-closing sales at its locations.
  • Cumulus Media, an audio entertainment company that operates 394 radio stations along with a radio syndication division and a podcast network, has filed for bankruptcy protection in Texas, Reuters reported. The filing includes a restructuring plan aimed at wiping out the majority of the company's $697 million debt.

For the previous edition of Deal Dispatch, click here.

Image: Edited by Benzinga using Shutterstock

data.title: BlackRock Blinked — Now Everyone Is Watching Blackstone, Apollo and KKR
data.description:
Private credit has become one of Wall Street's hottest trades. Now it may be facing its first real stress test.

BlackRock, Inc. (NYSE:BLK) recently limited withdrawals from its $26 billion HPS Corporate Lending Fund after redemption requests surged to roughly 9.3% of the fund's net asset value, reported The Financial Times. The asset manager approved about $620 million in redemptions, hitting a 5% quarterly threshold that allows the fund to restrict additional withdrawals.

The move highlights a structural tension inside the fast-growing private credit industry.

Private Credit's Liquidity Question

Semi-liquid private credit funds have attracted hundreds of billions of dollars from wealthy individuals and retail investors searching for higher yields.

But as redemptions rise, the industry's liquidity mismatch is drawing scrutiny. Investors can request withdrawals. The underlying loans, however, are designed to be held for years.

Now BlackRock's decision to cap withdrawals could put other public asset managers with large private credit businesses under closer scrutiny.

Wall Street's Lending Giants

Companies such as Blackstone, Apollo and KKR have expanded aggressively into private credit over the past decade.

The strategy has helped turn private lending into a trillion-dollar market. It filled the gap left when banks pulled back from corporate lending after the financial crisis.

For now, there are signs of divergence within the industry. Blackstone recently fulfilled all redemption requests from its own $82 billion private credit vehicle, even as withdrawals increased.

Still, BlackRock's move highlights a broader reality. Private credit may offer attractive yields. But liquidity — especially during periods of stress — is never guaranteed.

Photo: Photo Agency/Shutterstock
